meta.title: Earthquakes, Volcanoes, and Tsunamis
meta.author: Elizabeth Austin
meta.focusQuestion: What causes earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis, and how do they affect the world?

sections.title: Introduction
paragraphs: We usually think of the ground and the ocean as stable and peaceful things. The ground lies quietly beneath our feet, and the ocean laps gently against the shore. But forces deep within the Earth can suddenly be unleashed, destroying that peacefulness.

These forces cause the violent shakings of earthquakes; the explosions of ash, gases, and hot rocks of volcanoes; and the huge waves of tsunamis. In this book, you will read about these amazing events and the underlying forces that cause them.

sections.title: Deep Within the Earth
paragraphs: Beneath the soil, rock, and water on the surface of our planet, the Earth is constantly changing. The top layer of Earth is made of giant pieces of rock, like the pieces of a puzzle. These giant pieces of rock, called tectonic plates, make up the continents and ocean floors. There are fourteen major tectonic plates and many smaller plates that make up the outermost layer of the Earth's crust. These plates are around 100 km (60 mi) thick. Where the edges of the plates come together, there are often cracks and gaps, called faults. Most of the world's earthquakes and volcanic activity occur near the faults along the edges of the tectonic plates.

Underneath the plates, the Earth is very hot, with normal temperatures of 700 degrees C to 1300 degrees C (1,292 degrees F to 2,372 degrees F). It is so hot that rock melts into liquid magma. The plates float on top of the underlying magma. The magma is always moving, and as it moves it carries the plates around with it. Tectonic plates normally move at speeds of 0.65 to 8.50 centimeters (0.25 to 3.35 in) per year.

At the edges of the moving plates, three different things can happen. If the plates are moving against each other, one plate slides over or under the other plate. If the plates are moving past each other, the edges of the plates grind together. And if the plates are moving apart, they make a gap where magma seeps out.

sections.title: Earthquakes: Terrible Trembling
paragraphs: The edges of the tectonic plates usually move very slowly. But sometimes large pieces of the plates get caught on each other. The plates keep trying to move, but these large blocks of rock hold them back. The pressure and energy build up. Then, suddenly, the rocks give way, releasing all that pressure and energy. The plates jerk forward, and the ground shakes. Far above, people feel an earthquake.

Some earthquakes are small and harmless. In a small earthquake, the ground shakes a little, causing some hanging objects to swing. Tree branches sway, as if there were a gentle breeze. Some earthquakes are so small that we do not even notice them. Small earthquakes happen very often in certain parts of the world like California and Alaska in the United States, as well as in other countries like Chile, Greece, and Japan.
But sometimes the shaking is so strong that buildings crumble, bridges collapse, and large cracks open in the ground.
We measure the strength of earthquakes on an instrument called a seismograph. Each earthquake is given a number from the Richter scale, depending on its strength.

For each number on the Richter scale, an earthquake is ten times as strong as the previous number. A 6.0 earthquake is ten times as strong as a 5.0 earthquake. It is one hundred times as strong as a 4.0 earthquake! The largest earthquake ever recorded measured 9.5 on the Richter scale. It occurred on the coast of Chile in South America.

A major earthquake struck the San Francisco Bay Area of California on October 17, 1989. Known as the Loma Prieta Earthquake, it measured 6.9 to 7.1 magnitude on the Richter scale. Although the earthquake lasted only fifteen seconds, it killed more than sixty people and injured several thousand more. The earthquake also caused an estimated $6 billion in damage to buildings, roads, bridges, and other property. This was the largest earthquake to occur on the San Andreas Fault in California since 1906, when an even more powerful earthquake and the fires that followed it destroyed much of the city of San Francisco.

sections.title: Volcanoes: Enormous Explosions
paragraphs: A volcano occurs wherever magma from deep inside the Earth comes out through a crack in the surface. Volcanoes usually happen near the edges of the tectonic plates, where there are many cracks and thin spots where the magma can leak out. When the magma pours onto the surface, it hardens, often piling up into a mountain. Sometimes, the liquid rock flows peacefully out across the land. Many volcanoes on the Hawaiian Islands act like this.

But sometimes, in very powerful volcanoes, the hot magma cools before it gets to the surface. It hardens into a solid piece of rock, plugging up the crack or hole where it had been seeping out. It acts like a plug in a bottle. The magma continues to push upward. Hot gases in the magma press against the plug. The pressure gets greater and greater. Suddenly, the volcano explodes. Huge chunks of rock burst from the volcano. Entire mountainsides can be ripped away. Hot, poisonous gas, ash, and melted rock shoot into the sky. The volcano erupts with unbelievable power.

Many dangerous and destructive things happen during volcanic eruptions. When mountains explode, they send tons of rock rolling down their sides. These falling rocks are called landslides. Landslides can bury whole cities. They can block rivers, causing floods. Clouds of ash and dust rise into the air during volcanic eruptions. The ash falls to the ground like snow. The ash is so heavy that the roofs of houses can collapse under its weight. An ash cloud can spread all around the world. Large ash clouds can even block out sunlight. The temperature of the entire Earth can cool down after a large volcanic eruption.

A volcano can cause an explosion of extremely hot, poisonous gases. The explosion can be so powerful that it knocks over entire forests, and so hot that it starts destructive fires. Some large volcanoes have snow and ice on top. The hot gases melt all the snow and ice at once, and the water rushes down the mountain. This large, dirt-filled flood is called a mudslide. A mudslide is like a liquid landslide.

Scientists are not yet able to predict when earthquakes will happen. But fortunately they have gotten very good at predicting when a large volcano is about to erupt. In 1991, scientists warned the people living around a volcano in the country of the Philippines that the volcano was about to erupt. Many people left the area and found safety before the huge volcano erupted.

A very large volcano called Mount St. Helens erupted in Washington State on May 18, 1980. Mount St. Helens is part of a chain of volcanic mountains in the Cascade Mountain Range that runs throughout the Pacific Northwest where the Juan de Fuca tectonic plate meets the North American plate. The explosive power of the volcanic eruption completely destroyed the upper portions of the mountain and flattened all the trees in the forests for miles around. The resulting cloud of ash released into the atmosphere darkened the sky over a large part of the United States. The volcanic ash fell like snow for several hours in ten states following the eruption. The Mount St. Helens eruption was the deadliest in U.S. history, resulting in the deaths of fifty-seven people. It also destroyed homes, bridges, railroads, and highways.

sections.title: Tsunamis: Wild Waves
paragraphs: Tsunamis are huge waves caused by earthquakes or volcanoes. They used to be called "tidal waves." But the word "tidal" means something to do with the ocean's normal tides, and tsunamis have nothing to do with the tides. Tsunamis can be as high as a football field is long. They are the largest waves in the world.

The edges of the plates, where earthquakes and volcanoes often occur, usually lie near the edges of the oceans. The shaking of an earthquake or the explosion of a volcano can cause large landslides. Entire hills can collapse, and sometimes they fall into the sea and make huge waves. Earthquakes and volcanoes can also be found under the sea. Underwater earthquakes cause the sea floor to move violently. Undersea volcanoes cause explosions under the water. Both of these events create huge waves that spread across the surface of the ocean.
Tsunamis speed outward away from their source in all directions. A tsunami can cross an entire ocean. Tsunamis travel extremely fast — up to 320 kilometers per hour (200 mph).

In the open ocean, tsunamis may not be very high above the surface of the water, but they are very deep. As tsunamis approach land and move into shallow water, the elevated ocean floor pushes the wave upward. The wave slows to about 70 kilometers per hour (45 mph), but the ocean floor lifts it higher and higher. By the time a tsunami reaches land, it is tall enough to destroy almost anything in its path.

The largest tsunami ever recorded happened when an earthquake triggered a landslide in a small Alaskan bay in 1958. The landslide splashed water 524 meters (1,720 ft) into the air and sent a giant tsunami racing down the bay. Most tsunamis are not that big — they are usually between one and ten stories high when they reach the shore.

That might not sound very high, but it is hard to imagine the power of this much moving water. Most coastal towns and villages are much less than 30 meters (100 ft) above the sea. Even a small tsunami can destroy houses, streets, and entire towns. A tsunami caused by a volcano in Indonesia killed 36,000 people in 1883.

More recently, a large tsunami struck the countries bordering the Indian Ocean in 2004. The Indian Ocean Tsunami began when an earthquake occurred on the ocean floor near the western coast of Indonesia. The earthquake caused a ring of tsunami waves to speed outward from the point of origin, crossing hundreds and even thousands of kilometers of open ocean to strike countries far away. More than 225,000 people in eleven countries bordering the Indian Ocean were killed by the resulting waves. Satellite data analyzed after the tragedy showed that the height of the tsunami wave in the deep ocean was only 60 centimeters (2 ft). When the waves reached the shallow waters near the beaches, however, they often grew to reach over 24 meters (78 ft) tall. As a result of the 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami, many countries are now working together to create a tsunami warning system for that part of the world.

sections.title: Conclusion
paragraphs: Like volcanoes, tsunamis are becoming easier to predict. They usually do not hit the shore until a few minutes to a few hours after the earthquake or volcano that causes them. Scientists have instruments that detect volcanoes and earthquakes. When a large eruption or earthquake happens, scientists have time to warn people that a tsunami may be coming. Unfortunately, the system cannot warn people in time to save their homes or belongings. But they do have time to save their lives, which is the most important thing.

Our quiet planet occasionally turns violent. During a large earthquake, the ground shakes, destroying homes and property. In an explosive volcanic eruption, liquid rock, poisonous gases, ash, and landslides can bury cities. And tsunamis can cost billions of dollars in damage along coastlines. Scientists are trying to learn as much as they can about these violent events. As they learn more, scientists can predict volcanoes, earthquakes, and tsunamis in order to save thousands of lives.

vocabulary.definition: the shaking of Earth's crust caused by underground vibrations
vocabulary.sentence: The plates jerk forward, and the ground shakes. Far above, people feel an earthquake.

options: huge ocean waves caused by underwater volcanoes
explosions of ash and hot rock from deep inside Earth
the shaking of Earth's crust caused by underground vibrations
cracks in Earth's surface along tectonic plates

vocabulary.definition: cracks in Earth's crust along which movement occurs
vocabulary.sentence: Where the edges of the plates come together, there are often cracks and gaps, called faults.

options: cracks in Earth's surface along the edges of tectonic plates
pieces of rock that make up the continents
large ocean waves caused by earthquakes
instruments that measure earthquake strength

vocabulary.definition: melted, liquid rock beneath Earth's surface
vocabulary.sentence: It is so hot that rock melts into liquid magma.

options: giant pieces of rock that make up Earth's surface
cracks in the surface of the Earth
enormous ocean waves
melted, liquid rock beneath Earth's crust

vocabulary.definition: large sheets of rock and soil that make up Earth's crust
vocabulary.sentence: These giant pieces of rock, called tectonic plates, make up the continents and ocean floors.

options: large sheets of rock that make up Earth's crust
instruments used to measure earthquakes
volcanoes found under the ocean
layers of ice found near the Earth's poles

vocabulary.definition: enormous ocean waves caused by underwater earthquakes, landslides, or volcanoes
vocabulary.sentence: Tsunamis are huge waves caused by earthquakes or volcanoes.

options: explosions of hot gas and ash from volcanoes
cracks in the ocean floor where plates meet
enormous ocean waves caused by underwater earthquakes or volcanoes
moving masses of rock that flow down mountain slopes

vocabulary.definition: places on Earth's surface where gases, ash, and lava spew out
vocabulary.sentence: A volcano occurs wherever magma from deep inside the Earth comes out through a crack in the surface.

options: places where Earth's crust shakes violently
places on Earth's surface where gases, ash, and lava spew out
large ocean waves caused by earthquakes
instruments that detect underground vibrations

vocabulary.definition: moving masses of soil and rock that flow down slopes
vocabulary.sentence: When mountains explode, they send tons of rock rolling down their sides. These falling rocks are called landslides.

options: huge waves that crash onto the shore
explosions of gas and ash from volcanoes
moving masses of soil and rock flowing down slopes
cracks in Earth's crust where plates meet

vocabulary.definition: a dirt- and debris-filled flood of water
vocabulary.sentence: This large, dirt-filled flood is called a mudslide.

options: a type of volcanic explosion
a dirt- and debris-filled flood of water
a wave caused by an underwater earthquake
a crack in the Earth's surface

vocabulary.definition: the scale that measures the strength of earthquakes
vocabulary.sentence: Each earthquake is given a number from the Richter scale, depending on its strength.

options: the height of tsunami waves
the temperature of volcanic magma
the speed of tectonic plate movement
the strength of earthquakes

vocabulary.definition: an instrument that measures the strength of earthquakes
vocabulary.sentence: We measure the strength of earthquakes on an instrument called a seismograph.

options: an instrument that measures earthquake strength
a warning system for tsunamis
a scale that measures volcanic eruptions
a satellite that tracks plate movement

questions.question: How is a tsunami different from an earthquake?
questions.evidence: Tsunamis are huge waves caused by earthquakes or volcanoes.
choices: A tsunami is a huge wave caused by an earthquake or volcano
A tsunami causes an earthquake under the ocean
A tsunami is measured on the Richter scale
A tsunami does not cause any damage to property
questions.explanation: A tsunami is a huge wave CAUSED BY an earthquake or volcano — it's the effect, not the cause. Tsunamis have nothing to do with tides.

questions.question: What does the Richter scale measure?
questions.evidence: We measure the strength of earthquakes on an instrument called a seismograph. Each earthquake is given a number from the Richter scale, depending on its strength.
choices: the amount of magma released by a volcano
the strength of earthquakes
the speed at which tectonic plates move
the height of tsunami waves
questions.explanation: The Richter scale measures the strength of earthquakes. Each number is ten times stronger than the previous one — a 6.0 is ten times stronger than a 5.0!

questions.question: What is magma?
questions.evidence: It is so hot that rock melts into liquid magma. The plates float on top of the underlying magma.
choices: giant pieces of rock that make up the surface of the Earth
cracks in the surface of the Earth where plates meet
enormous ocean waves caused by underwater eruptions
melted, liquid rock beneath Earth's crust
questions.explanation: Magma is melted, liquid rock beneath Earth's surface. It's so hot (700-1300 degrees C) that solid rock melts into it. Tectonic plates float on top of magma.

questions.question: Which word means cracks in the surface of the Earth, usually around the edges of plates?
questions.evidence: Where the edges of the plates come together, there are often cracks and gaps, called faults.
choices: faults
plates
mudslides
earthquakes
questions.explanation: Faults are cracks and gaps where tectonic plate edges come together. Most earthquakes and volcanic activity occur near these faults.

questions.question: Which of the following states a FACT?
questions.evidence: A fact can be proven true or false. An opinion expresses a belief or feeling. "The Earth is constantly changing" is a fact supported by evidence about tectonic plates.
choices: Earthquakes aren't dangerous
Earthquakes are easy to predict
The Earth is constantly changing
Volcanoes never cause damage
questions.explanation: "The Earth is constantly changing" is a fact — it can be proven by evidence about tectonic plates moving, magma flowing, etc. The other statements are false opinions.

questions.question: Which of the following states an OPINION?
questions.evidence: An opinion expresses a belief or feeling and cannot be proven. A fact can be proven true or false.
choices: Tsunamis are the largest waves in the world
Clouds of dust and ash rise into the air during a volcanic eruption
Underwater earthquakes cause the sea floor to move violently
People shouldn't worry about earthquakes
questions.explanation: "People shouldn't worry" is an opinion — it expresses a belief. The other three are facts that can be proven with evidence.

questions.question: Why is it difficult to see tsunamis in the open ocean?
questions.evidence: In the open ocean, tsunamis may not be very high above the surface of the water, but they are very deep.
choices: They only happen close to the shore
They haven't built up enough strength yet
They travel too fast to be seen
They are deep, but not high above the surface
questions.explanation: In the open ocean, tsunamis are very DEEP (extending far below the surface) but not very HIGH above the water. They only grow tall when they reach shallow water near land.

questions.question: How are earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis alike?
questions.evidence: But forces deep within the Earth can suddenly be unleashed... These forces cause the violent shakings of earthquakes; the explosions of ash, gases, and hot rocks of volcanoes; and the huge waves of tsunamis.
choices: All are caused by activity deep in the Earth
All cause a drop in the Earth's temperature
All give off poisonous gases
All occur only along the Pacific Ocean
questions.explanation: All three are caused by forces deep within the Earth — specifically by the movement of tectonic plates and the magma beneath them.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a possible result of a tsunami?
questions.evidence: Tsunamis destroy houses, streets, and entire towns. People are killed. But tectonic plates getting stuck is a CAUSE of earthquakes, not a result of tsunamis.
choices: People are killed
Tectonic plates get stuck
Entire towns are destroyed
Houses are destroyed
questions.explanation: Tectonic plates getting stuck is a CAUSE of earthquakes — not a RESULT of tsunamis. Tsunamis result from earthquakes/volcanoes, and then destroy houses, towns, and kill people.

questions.question: What is the main idea of this book?
questions.evidence: The book covers earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis — what causes them, how they work, and their devastating effects. The introduction states: forces deep within the Earth can suddenly be unleashed, destroying that peacefulness.
choices: One day, scientists will be able to warn people before earthquakes occur
Volcanic explosions can be so powerful that they knock over entire forests
Earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis are some of the most destructive forces of nature
Even small tsunamis can destroy houses
questions.explanation: The main idea covers ALL three phenomena — earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis — and how they are all destructive forces caused by deep Earth activity. The other options are just details about one phenomenon.

questions.question: In this book, a volcano was compared to a bottle of soda. Explain this comparison. Why do you think scientists are getting better at predicting volcanoes but not earthquakes?
questions.explanation: A volcano is like a soda bottle with a plug: magma pushes against the plug (like gas in a bottle), pressure builds, and eventually it explodes. For the second part, think about what signs a volcano might show before erupting.
opinionPrompts: How is a volcano like a bottle with a plug?
What signs might a volcano show before erupting?
Why are earthquakes harder to predict than volcanoes?

prompts.K: What do you already know about earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis?
prompts.W: What do you want to learn about earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis?
prompts.L: What did you learn from reading this book?
prompts.S: What do you still want to know about earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis?

L: Tectonic plates are giant pieces of rock that make up Earth's crust and float on magma.
Earthquakes happen when tectonic plates get caught on each other and suddenly jerk forward.
The Richter scale measures earthquake strength — each number is 10 times stronger than the previous one.
Volcanoes explode when magma cools and plugs up the opening, building pressure until it bursts.
Tsunamis are huge waves caused by underwater earthquakes or volcanoes — they grow taller in shallow water.
Scientists can predict volcanoes and tsunamis but not yet earthquakes.

intro.title: Fact vs. Opinion
intro.content: FACT: The ground seems stable. FACT: Forces deep in Earth cause earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis. These are VERIFIABLE with evidence!

deepWithin.title: Tectonic Plates
deepWithin.content: Earth's crust = puzzle pieces! 14 major plates + smaller ones. They're 100 km thick and FLOAT on magma beneath them!

deepWithin.title: Magma & Faults
deepWithin.content: Magma = melted rock under Earth's surface (700-1300 degrees C!). Faults = cracks where plates meet. MOST earthquakes/volcanoes happen at faults!

deepWithin.title: 3 Things at Plate Edges
deepWithin.content: 1) Plates move AGAINST each other → one slides under. 2) Plates slide PAST each other → edges grind. 3) Plates move APART → magma seeps out.

earthquakes.title: Richter Scale
earthquakes.content: Each number = 10x stronger! 5.0 → 6.0 = 10x. 4.0 → 6.0 = 100x! Largest ever: 9.5 in Chile. Seismograph = instrument that measures it.

earthquakes.title: Cause & Effect
earthquakes.content: Cause: Plates get caught on each other, pressure builds. Effect: Rocks give way, plates jerk forward, ground shakes = EARTHQUAKE!

earthquakes.title: Loma Prieta 1989
earthquakes.content: 6.9-7.1 magnitude. Only 15 seconds long! But: 60+ killed, thousands injured, $6 billion damage. Shows how powerful even brief quakes are.

volcanoes.title: Landslides & Mudslides
volcanoes.content: Landslide = tons of rock rolling down mountain. Mudslide = dirt-filled flood (like liquid landslide!). Both can bury cities!

volcanoes.title: Volcano = Bottle with Plug
volcanoes.content: Magma cools → plugs the hole (like bottle cap). More magma pushes up → pressure builds → BOOM! Explosion! This is a KEY comparison in the text.

volcanoes.title: Mt. St. Helens 1980
volcanoes.content: Deadliest U.S. eruption: 57 died. Destroyed mountaintop, flattened forests for miles, ash fell in 10 states. Juan de Fuca plate meets North American plate!

tsunamis.title: Tsunami vs. Tidal Wave
tsunamis.content: NOT the same! Tidal = normal ocean tides. Tsunami = caused by earthquakes/volcanoes. They have NOTHING to do with tides!

tsunamis.title: How Tsunamis Grow
tsunamis.content: Open ocean: deep but NOT high (maybe only 2 ft tall!). Approaching shore: ocean floor PUSHES wave UPWARD. Slows to 45 mph but grows to 78+ ft!

tsunamis.title: 2004 Indian Ocean Tsunami
tsunamis.content: Earthquake near Indonesia → ring of waves. Deep ocean: only 2 ft tall. Near shore: 78+ ft. 225,000+ killed in 11 countries. Led to warning system!

conclusion.title: Predicting the Unpredictable
conclusion.content: Can predict: volcanoes (they show signs), tsunamis (minutes-hours after quake). CAN'T predict: earthquakes (no warning signs yet). Scientists still learning!

conclusion.title: Main Idea
conclusion.content: Our quiet planet occasionally turns violent. But as scientists learn more → better predictions → save thousands of lives!

grammar.title: Commas in a Series & Compound Words
grammar.explanation: When you list three or more things, use commas to separate them. Without commas, the sentence is hard to read! Example: "gas, ash, and rock" — the commas show these are three separate things.
rules: When listing 3+ items, put a comma between each item: A, B, and C
The comma before "and" (the Oxford comma) helps avoid confusion
Without commas: "Hot poisonous gas ash and melted rock" — this sounds like one giant thing!
With commas: "Hot, poisonous gas, ash, and melted rock" — now we see these are separate items
Compound words: two words joined to make a new word (land + slide = landslide)

wordBuilding.base: buildings crumble bridges collapse and cracks open
wordBuilding.answer: buildings crumble, bridges collapse, and cracks open

exercises.sentence: But sometimes the shaking is so strong that buildings crumble, bridges collapse, and large cracks open in the ground.
answers: large cracks open in the ground
exercises.hint: Find the 3 things that happen during a strong earthquake. Where are the commas?

exercises.sentence: Hot, poisonous gas, ash, and melted rock shoot into the sky.
exercises.hint: What 3 things shoot into the sky during a volcanic eruption?

exercises.question: Which sentence uses commas correctly in a series?
options: The book covers earthquakes volcanoes and tsunamis.
The book covers earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis.
The book covers, earthquakes volcanoes and, tsunamis.
The book covers earthquakes and volcanoes, and tsunamis.
exercises.explanation: When listing 3+ items, put a comma between each: "earthquakes, volcanoes, and tsunamis".

exercises.question: Where should commas be added? "Scientists measured temperature moisture wind speed and direction."
options: Scientists measured, temperature moisture, wind speed and direction.
Scientists measured temperature, moisture, wind speed, and direction.
Scientists, measured temperature moisture wind, speed and direction.
No commas needed.
exercises.explanation: The list has 4 items: temperature, moisture, wind speed, and direction. Add commas between each.

exercises.sentence: Add commas: "They also destroyed homes bridges railroads and highways."
options: They also destroyed homes, bridges, railroads, and highways.
They also destroyed homes bridges, railroads and highways.
They also, destroyed homes bridges railroads, and highways.
They also destroyed, homes, bridges railroads and highways.
exercises.explanation: List of 4 things destroyed: homes, bridges, railroads, and highways — comma between each.

exercises.sentence: Add commas: "Beneath the soil rock and water on the surface of our planet, the Earth is constantly changing."
options: Beneath the soil, rock, and water on the surface of our planet, the Earth is constantly changing.
Beneath the soil rock, and water on the surface, of our planet the Earth is constantly changing.
Beneath, the soil rock and water, on the surface of our planet, the Earth is constantly changing.
Beneath the soil rock and water, on the surface of our planet the Earth, is constantly changing.
exercises.explanation: "Soil, rock, and water" is a list of 3 things — comma between each.
Land (ground/earth) + slide (slip down) = landslide (mass of rock sliding down a mountain)!
Mud (wet dirt) + slide (flow down) = mudslide (a dirt-filled flood flowing down a mountain)!

options: Yes — it is made of two common English words
No — it comes from Greek words (seismo = earthquake, graph = write)
Yes — it is made of "sea" and "graph"
No — it is just one word with no parts
exercises.explanation: "Seismograph" comes from Greek: seismo (earthquake) + graph (to write/record). It is NOT a compound of two English words.
